motions.title: Potholes & Highway Maintenance
motions.text: County Council notes the thousands of potholes plaguing Lancashire; the impact on pedestrians, cyclists and vehicles; the current crisis caused through problems with the highway defect reporting system and back-office systems; consequent absence of reliable publicly available information; the impact of blocked and inadequate drainage systems; the cabinet's review of the Highway Safety Inspection Policy in January; the failure to meet response times during 2026; LCC's rating as 'Amber' in the DfT's road maintenance ratings; that the 2027 Tour De France requires a contribution from the Highways Maintenance Capital Programme and revenue; the cabinet member's acknowledgement that resources for repairing and resurfacing roads and pavements is woefully inadequate. Council also notes that last May, the Reform administration was elected on a promise to fill every pothole.
resolves: In view of the dangerous and deplorable condition of our local roads and pavements, the Chief Executive request the Secretary of State for Transport, Chancellor of the Exchequer and Prime Minister to increase funding for highway maintenance in Lancashire
Cabinet is requested to review prioritisation of funding for highway repairs within LCC's overall capital programme, and prioritisation within the Highways block

Overview and Scrutiny is requested to review highway repair and resurfacing systems and practices: pothole repair practices; operation of Highway Safety Inspections; operation of highway defect reporting systems; and, criteria and allocations determined through the Highway Asset Management Plan
motions.recommended_action: Amend — accept Resolve 1 (strengthened with 31:1 funding disparity data), rewrite Resolve 2 (annual progress report instead of opposition-directed review), rewrite Resolve 3 (note Whipp already sits on scrutiny committee — exposes motion as political theatre)

motions.title: Election Integrity / Family Voting
motions.text: With elections taking place in 6 districts in Lancashire in May, this council is concerned to note reports of 'family voting' at the recent Gorton and Denton parliamentary byelection made by the independent election observers 'Democracy Volunteers'. This council condemns the practice and other election offences, such as postal vote harvesting or other misuse of postal voting, and expresses concern about the implications for a free and fair democracy.
resolves: The Chief Executive write to each Returning Officer in Lancashire to highlight concerns and request reassurance on steps being taken at the May elections to ensure the law is enforced in relation to family voting, including full engagement with Democracy Volunteers or other accredited election observer organisations
The Chief Executive write to the Secretary of State expressing concerns and seeking assurance on policing and law enforcement at polling stations
All Lancashire MPs and Council Leaders be sent copies of the correspondence and invited to demonstrate their support for fair elections
motions.recommended_action: Pass on division — force recorded vote to put every councillor on record

motions.title: Youth Democracy Engagement
motions.text: Council notes the importance of engaging young people in politics and public service to ensure a healthy, representative, and forward-looking democracy in Lancashire; that Government intends to lower the voting age to 16 in time for the next general election.
resolves: Ask Cabinet to develop a strategy to enhance young people's involvement in democracy and politics, including organising campaigns to encourage young people to register to vote
Inviting young people to engage with council meetings
Promoting political education and engagement in collaboration with local educators
Organising events such as the Great Lancashire Debate
Creating more opportunities for the Youth Council and Youth Parliament representatives to engage with the council
motions.recommended_action: Friendly amendment — add existing initiatives (Youth Council, YP already engaged) to demonstrate alignment while taking ownership

motions.title: Drug Legalisation Opposition
motions.text: In Lancashire, we have seen firsthand the devastating effects that drugs have on individuals, families and communities. This Council has previously recognised the need for additional funding, improved treatment provision, and stronger prevention measures. In light of the recent comments by the Leader of the Green Party, Zack Polanski, who agreed with a Kent Green councillor calling for the legalisation of all drugs including class A drugs like heroin and crack cocaine, this Council opposes any dilution of drugs law.
resolves: Instruct the Chief Executive to write to the Home Secretary setting out this council's condemnation of the Green Party views, opposition to legalisation/decriminalisation, and request that local authorities be consulted before changes to drug legislation
Share that correspondence with all Lancashire MPs and Council Leaders requesting their comments on the impact of legalisation and decriminalisation
motions.recommended_action: Pass on division — force recorded vote to expose Green/progressive position on drugs

member_questions.answerer_role: Cabinet Member for Data, Technology, Customer and Efficiency
member_questions.question: What changes have been made since May to commissioning, procurement and contract management and could the Cabinet Member give his assessment of the state of those services in May, now and where they will be moving forwards?

We understand there will be an announcement due concerning the future of Lancashire's care homes and day care centres. There is still huge uncertainty for residents and workers. Lancashire Labour Group have proudly stood behind UNISON in their campaign to prevent closure, outsourcing or privatisation of all facilities. Will County Councillor Dalton take the opportunity to reassure residents all existing facilities will remain in public hands and the day care centres we currently operate will remain open?

member_questions.answerer_role: Cabinet Member for Highways and Transport
member_questions.question: In March 2025 following the addition by the Conservative administration of £45m over 3 years to the Highways Budget, Cabinet approved an indicative programme of 141 schemes at almost £21.5m, plus a further 41 unfunded priority schemes at almost £6m. Since May 2025, how many of these schemes have been delivered as originally approved and how many have been removed from each programme?

member_questions.answerer_role: Cabinet Member for Community and Cultural Services
member_questions.question: Does the Cabinet Member agree that abundant, safe, clean water is now under threat in Lancashire and across the country, and that Lancashire County Council could via its public health powers and community leadership role begin to advocate for more action to tackle water course pollution, supply failure and drought?

The Minister of State for Care, Stephen Kinnock MP, recently wrote to LCC welcoming the public consultation on care services and trusting that LCC will carefully consider the results. Would the Cabinet Member for Adult Services wish to comment?

public_questions.question: Disabled people in Lancashire used to pay £1 to travel on the bus before 9.30am during the week, but now it is £3 or £3.50. Members of the Lancashire Learning Disability Peer Advocacy Network have lives that begin before 9.30am. Are you willing to work with us to come up with a better, fairer solution for disabled people travelling before 9.30am on weekdays in Lancashire? What are you going to do differently to show that you are really listening to the voices of disabled people?
public_questions.answerer_role: Cabinet Member for Highways and Transport

public_questions.question: Does the Cabinet Member recognise the important role that farm shops play in rural community life, local food supply chains, and the rural economy? Does the Cabinet Member agree that poor provision for pedestrians and cyclists reflects legacy infrastructure challenges rather than recent policy decisions, and can they confirm the Council is now taking a more considered and evidence-led approach?
public_questions.answerer_role: Cabinet Member for Highways and Transport

enriched_agenda.description: 2 public questions: disabled bus fares (high emotive value), farm shop rural roads (friendly)
5 member questions: procurement (Reform, friendly), care homes x2 (Labour attack, Reform support), highways capital (Conservative attack), water (Green, low risk)
enriched_agenda.standing_order: SO B30 — max 6 questions, 1 supplementary each

enriched_agenda.item: Recommendations of the Independent Remuneration Panel - Members' Allowance Scheme 2026/27
enriched_agenda.description: Opposition group leader allowances restructured. Main opposition leader retains £23,597; opposition deputy reduced from 27.5% to 20% (£8,581). Smaller groups: Con £3,400, LD £2,500, Lab £2,200, OWL £1,600. Public sensitivity: councillors voting on their own pay.

enriched_agenda.description: Cabinet decisions for noting. Check for any contentious items.

4 motions: 1) Potholes (CRITICAL — LibDem attack on highways), 2) Election integrity (Reform, pass cleanly), 3) Youth democracy (Independent, medium), 4) Drug legalisation (Reform, pass cleanly). Total time: 120 minutes.
enriched_agenda.standing_order: SO B37 — max 4 motions, 250 words, 30 min per motion, 120 min total
